last_result.message: Choose the committed tactic you want to rewind before.

checkpoint_options.label: Before latest call-obligation tactic #16
checkpoint_options.why_checkpoint: call-obligation local boundary; selecting it keeps the opened call obligation and removes later local work
checkpoint_options.repair_use_when: Use this when broad inlining exposed too much code, hid a call handle, or a targeted inline would keep the proof frontier cleaner.
checkpoint_options.after_rewind_next: Probe targeted `inline{1}`/`inline{2}` or inspect `call_site_options` before expanding more code.
checkpoint_options.effect_if_selected: This will undo committed tactic #16 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

checkpoint_options.label: After call opened / before obligation work #6
checkpoint_options.why_checkpoint: call-local boundary; selecting it keeps the call tactic and removes work committed after the call opened
checkpoint_options.repair_use_when: Use this only when the chosen procedure-level route was wrong, not for a local invariant or branch-surgery problem.
checkpoint_options.after_rewind_next: Re-enter the procedure with a different high-level route or inspect `goal_info` first.
checkpoint_options.effect_if_selected: This will undo committed tactic #6 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

checkpoint_options.label: Before call invariant #5
checkpoint_options.committed_tactic: call (_: inv_cpa SplitC2.I1.RO.m{1} SplitC2.I2.RO.m{1} Mem.log{1} Mem.log{2} Mem.lc{1} Mem.lc{2} BNR.lenc{1} BNR.lenc{2} BNR.ndec{1} BNR.ndec{2}).
checkpoint_options.why_checkpoint: call invariant introduction point; later oracle/branch obligations often reveal missing preserved facts or an over-strong equality from this tactic
checkpoint_options.repair_use_when: Use this when the current blocker is an invariant gap, a bad-event fact that should have been preserved, or a local surgery problem after a call. Prefer this over fresh restart when the top-level route still looks right.
checkpoint_options.after_rewind_next: Inspect `call_subgoals` or `subgoal_gap`, then probe a revised `call (_: ...)` invariant or a smaller prefix.
checkpoint_options.effect_if_selected: This will undo committed tactic #5 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

lines: &1 (left ) : {k : key, n : nonce, c2 : bytes, a : associated_data,
p2 : message, nap : nonce * associated_data * message, t : tag,
p, p0, p1 : plaintext, c, c0, c1 : ciphertext}
&2 (right) : {n : nonce, c1 : bytes, t : poly_out, a : associated_data,
p1 : message, nap : nonce * associated_data * message,
p, p0 : plaintext, c, c0 : ciphertext}
inv_cpa SplitC2.I1.RO.m{1} SplitC2.I2.RO.m{1} Mem.log{1} Mem.log{2} Mem.
lc{1} Mem.lc{2} BNR.lenc{1} BNR.lenc{2} BNR.ndec{1} BNR.ndec{2}) /\

moves.applicability: This tactic shape still has placeholders; replace them with concrete EasyCrypt arguments from the current goal before probing or committing it.
moves.runnable_status: Not yet; replace every placeholder with concrete EasyCrypt terms from the current goal before probing or committing this tactic.
missing_input: Concrete EasyCrypt arguments for every placeholder in the displayed tactic.
how_to_complete: Use the current goal variables, hypotheses, precondition, postcondition, and visible program statements to fill the placeholders.

named_handles.why_visible: This lemma-call shape is not a complete tactic yet; determine the missing module, procedure, or term arguments from the current goal before probing it.
limitations: The tactic shape is hidden until the handle is callable at the current frontier.

frontier_blockers.left: 2 setup statement(s): c1 <- (n, a, c2, t); c0 <- c1
frontier_blockers.effect: Residual statements remain after the live call frontier; their effects are not part of the callee obligation.

action.tactic_shape: seq 5 0 : <invariant preserving the call lemma postcondition>.
action.reason: the matching call is live but no paired edit-script step was available; expose this side's call frontier before calling the lemma

named_call_templates.tactic_shape: exlim n{2}, ROin.m{1}, ROout.m{1} => n0 mr0 ms0; call (equ_cc n0 mr0 ms0).

lifted_value_arguments.reason: lemma equality binds value slot `n0` to projection `arg{2}.`1` of the current call argument
lemma equality binds value slot `mr0` to state expression `ROin.m{1}`
lemma equality binds value slot `ms0` to state expression `ROout.m{1}`

lemma_precondition_coverage.strategy_boundary: Static coverage only: it tells the prover what facts the call will need, but it does not prove those facts or rank the proof strategy by lemma name.

rows.left: 2 setup statement(s): c1 <- (n, a, c2, t); c0 <- c1
rows.proof_read: These statements remain after the live call frontier. Their effects are checked after the call returns; a bare call obligation is checked before these residual statements execute.

residual_frontier.effect: These statements remain after the live call frontier and are checked after the call returns.
limitations: The surface reports suffix structure only; it does not choose whether to use a call, inline, wp, or another local tactic.

call_sites.statement: c2 <@ ChaCha( CCRO(SplitD. RO_DOM(SplitC1. RO_Pair(SplitC2. RO_Pair(SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2. RO), SplitD.ROF.RO))).enc(k, n, p2)
t <@ Poly( CCRO(SplitD. RO_DOM(SplitC1. RO_Pair(SplitC2. RO_Pair(SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2. RO), SplitD.ROF.RO))).mac(k, n, a, c2)

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: setup statements appear before the current frontier; sampling steps are visible inside the frontier; call sites are visible at the frontier; residual statements remain after a call frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.left_first: no matching left-side call at this frontier
first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: The visible frontier is one-sided; use a one-sided tactic, alignment step, or seq cut before applying a symmetric tactic.

rows.left: 4 setup statement(s): p1 <- p0; k <- Mem.k; nap <- p1; ... (1 more)
rows.right: 2 setup statement(s): nap <- p0; (n, a, p1) <- nap
rows.proof_read: These are setup statements before the current frontier; preserve the proof-relevant initialized state in later invariants, seq cuts, or calls.

rows.left: no matching left-side call at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a call frontier; compare the procedures/oracle wrappers before choosing a named call or invariant call.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

right.note: count from frontier alignment; the body parser under-counted the asymmetric (right-only) setup statements (0 parsed)

absorb_depth.sp_hint: sp 4 2 / wp -4 -2 absorbs the straight-line setup (4 left, 2 right) before the next frontier

left.statement: c2 <@ ChaCha( CCRO(SplitD. RO_DOM(SplitC1. RO_Pair(SplitC2. RO_Pair(SplitC2.I1.RO, SplitC2.I2.RO), SplitC1.I2. RO), SplitD.ROF.RO))).enc(k, n, p2)

application_context.how_to_use: Use this section when constructing the concrete invariant inside `call (_: ...)`. This section is not a tactic. It is a checklist for constructing the concrete invariant before using `probe_tactic` or `commit_tactic`.

inspect_if_unsure.why: After you draft a concrete invariant, this preview shows the obligations it creates and can reveal missing facts or extra conjuncts that make the proof harder.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

notes.message: proof-state analysis sees live named call handle(s), but none is currently at EasyCrypt's last-call frontier (chacha_enc1, chacha_enc2, equ_cc, poly_mac1). `call <lemma>` may fail until wp/seq/targeted transforms expose the matching call as the last statement.

proof-state analysis layer is call_site: 3 call site(s) and 5 callable lemma handle(s) are live (chacha_enc1, chacha_enc2, equ_cc, poly_mac1). `inline *` is a broad global transformation here and can hide those handles; named calls and targeted inline are route context...

One-sided sampling residual: t{2} <$ dpoly_out. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The visible frontier contains call sites or named equiv handles may apply.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are at an abstract-adversary `call (_: <inv>)` and want the mechanical glob frame of the invariant before adding your own semantic conjuncts.
ask_manager_for.returns: the mechanical `={glob ...}` frame of the call invariant (shared abstract oracle modules, daemon-confirmed to apply and spawn the obligations). When a named coupling predicate fits the in-scope state it ALSO surfaces a field-threaded carrier (`inv … s1{1} s2{2}`), plus candidate inductive side-conditions (`dom ⊆ used-set`) for any random oracle the call queries. All revisable starting points — confirm the coupling and add/adjust the semantic conjuncts (key/state correspondences, set-membership).

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: A tactic has multiple EasyCrypt argument forms.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: The frontier may need indexed `sp i j` before branch or call tactics.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery

ask_manager_for.use_when: LHS/RHS statement order may need swap/alignment context.
ask_manager_for.returns: swap/alignment context for the visible pRHL frontier

lookup_candidates.use_when: Resolve exact signature for `chacha_enc1` only when this handle route is being tested.
Resolve exact signature for `chacha_enc2` only when this handle route is being tested.
Resolve exact signature for `poly_mac1` only when this handle route is being tested.
Resolve exact signature for `poly_mac2` only when this handle route is being tested.
